title: Teaching
text:
6 Credits | Winter Semester
Course Information | Materials


This course provides comprehensive coverage of multimedia technologies and applications. Students learn both theoretical foundations and practical skills in creating and processing multimedia content.




Multimedia Content Creation - Principles and best practices
2D & 3D Graphics - Design, modeling, and rendering
Video Processing - Editing, encoding, and streaming
Audio Processing - Recording, editing, and effects
Web Technologies - HTML5, CSS3, JavaScript
3D Web Graphics - VRML, X3D, WebGL
Interactive Media - User interfaces and interactivity





Test: 10 points
Project: 40 points
Final Exam: 50 points

The course combines lectures with hands-on laboratory practice, allowing students to develop practical skills alongside theoretical knowledge.

text:
Research Areas
My research projects focus on:

Software Visualization

Understanding software dependencies and evolution

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:
Abstract
In a complete theory there is an element corresponding to each element of reality. A sufficient condition for the reality of a physical quantity is the possibility of predicting it with certainty, without disturbing the system. In quantum mechanics in the case of two physical quantities described by non-commuting operators, the knowledge of one precludes the knowledge of the other. Then either (1) the description of reality given by the wave function in quantum mechanics is not complete or (2) these two quantities cannot have simultaneous reality. Consideration of the problem of making predictions concerning a system on the basis of measurements made on another system that had previously interacted with it leads to the result that if (1) is false then (2) is also false. One is thus led to conclude that the description of reality as given by a wave function is not complete.

title: Can quantum-mechanical description of physical reality be considered complete?
text:

title: Lectures
text:
Credits: 6 | Semester: Winter
This course provides comprehensive coverage of multimedia technologies and applications. Students learn both theoretical foundations and practical skills in creating and processing multimedia content.



Multimedia content creation principles
2D graphics design and editing
Image representation and formats
Video processing and compression (MPEG standards)
Audio encoding and processing

Hypertext markup languages (HTML5)
Multimedia composition and integration





Test: 10 points
Project: 40 points (semester project involving 2D/3D graphics, audio/video processing)
Final Exam: Required for completion
Minimum to Pass: 56 points total





Lectures: 3 hours per week
Seminars: 2 hours per week
Study Type: Full-time, attendance method

title: Supervised Theses
text:
Over the years, I have supervised more than 40 bachelor’s and master’s thesis projects across various topics in computer science, with a focus on visualization, graphics, and software engineering.


The supervised theses cover a wide range of topics including:

Software Visualization - 3D visualization of software architecture and dependencies
Graph Algorithms - Layout algorithms, clustering, and edge bundling techniques
Augmented Reality - AR applications for data and software visualization
Interactive Visualization - Web-based and desktop visualization tools
Data Mining - Visual data mining and analytics
Multimedia - Video processing, 3D graphics, and multimedia systems
Web Technologies - HTML5, WebGL, and interactive web applications

title: Supervised Theses
text:
Current Opportunities
I am always looking for motivated students interested in: - Software visualization and visual analytics - 3D graphics and augmented reality - Graph algorithms and network visualization - Interactive data visualization - Web-based visualization tools
If you’re interested in working on a thesis project in these areas, please contact me to discuss potential topics.

What I Look For

Strong programming skills (Java, JavaScript, Python, C++)
Interest in visualization and graphics
Motivation to explore new technologies
Ability to work independently
Good communication skills

title: Research & Publications
text:
My research focuses on innovative approaches to understanding and visualizing complex software systems and data structures.


Software Visualization in 3D Space Developing techniques for visualizing software architecture, dependencies, and evolution in three-dimensional environments to provide better understanding of complex systems.
Graph Layout & Visualization Algorithms Creating efficient algorithms for laying out and visualizing large graphs and networks, including edge bundling techniques to reduce visual clutter.
Interactive Data Visualization & Visual Data Mining Exploring interactive methods for data exploration and mining, enabling users to discover patterns and insights in complex datasets.
Augmented Reality Applications Investigating the use of augmented reality for photorealistic data visualization and software structure representation in real-world contexts.
Software Evolution & Metrics Visualization Analyzing and visualizing how software systems evolve over time, including metrics visualization and program execution dynamics.
Documentation Generation from Source Code Automated approaches to generate and visualize software documentation directly from source code analysis.

title: Research & Publications
text:
Supervised Research

Thesis Supervision
I have supervised over 40 bachelor’s and master’s thesis projects (2007-2016+) in areas including:

Software visualization and 3D graphics
Graph layout algorithms
Augmented reality applications
Interactive data visualization
Software metrics and evolution analysis
Multimedia content visualization

Recent thesis topics include: - Photorealistic data visualization in augmented reality - Graph clustering and edge bundling algorithms - Program execution visualization - Dynamic object-oriented program animation - Interactive 3D browsing systems
For more details, see the Supervised Theses section.
